aliases: Peking Roaster Duck Rice 北京烤鸭饭

english: Peking Roaster Duck Rice 北京烤鸭饭

region_of_origin: Singapore (Chinese-origin dish, Beijing culinary tradition adapted for Singapore hawker context)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Peking roast duck rice. FOODON_00002572 (cooked rice dish) is used as the closest broader class. A more specific term for Chinese roast duck rice dishes is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Peking roast duck rice. A0CKP (rice-based dish with meat) is the closest defensible broader anchor. Specificity is limited to the category level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Peking roast duck rice or equivalent Chinese rice dish. SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not extend to this level of dish specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Peking roast duck rice as served in a Singapore hawker centre or Chinese restaurant, comprising approximately 200g steamed white rice topped with 3–4 slices of roast duck and accompanied by hoisin sauce, cucumber, and spring onion, weighing approximately 350g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Peking Roaster Duck Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010917 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g macro and micronutrient values are taken directly from this HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry; it is estimated at approximately 1.2g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for roast duck with skin (USDA SR Legacy #05140) proportionally adjusted for the rice-to-duck ratio in this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for white rice-based mixed dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Peking roast duck rice as a composite dish. GI of 68 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for steamed white rice (GI ~72, medium-high) adjusted downward to approximately 68 to account for the protein content (6.5g/100g from duck) and dietary fibre (3.7g/100g) which are established moderators of glycaemic response per Atkinson et al. (2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (68 × 25.19g × 350g/100) / 100 = 68 × 88.2g / 100 ≈ 59.9 / 100 × serving fraction; simplified GL per 350g serving = (68 × 25.19 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 59.9 → rounded to 11.8 per 100g-equivalent serving portion for comparability. Serving GL = (68 × (25.19 × 3.5) / 100) = (68 × 0.8817) = 59.9 / 5.07 ≈ 11.8 per standard 175g carbohydrate-contributing portion. Estimate is conservative and should be replaced with direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g; a 350g serving delivers approximately 809mg sodium, representing 35–40% of the WHO daily limit of 2000mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request sauce served on the side to reduce sodium intake, limit portion size to one standard plate, and note that the potassium content (327mg/100g) partially offsets the sodium load in line with DASH-diet principles, but cumulative sodium from hoisin and soy-based sauces remains a meaningful concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19g/100g with estimated GI of 68 (medium); a 350g serving delivers approximately 88g of carbohydrate, which is a substantial glucose load for a single meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a standard plate (approximately 175g), request reduced hoisin sauce to minimise added sugars, and note that the dietary fibre (3.7g/100g) and protein (6.5g/100g) content moderates the glycaemic response compared to plain white rice, making this a preferable rice dish choice when portion-controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat approximately 1.2g/100g from duck skin; sodium 231mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0mg/100g in HPB source (likely an underestimate for a duck-containing dish).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request duck served without skin to reduce saturated fat intake, choose reduced-sodium sauce options, and note that the dietary fibre (3.7g/100g) and potassium (327mg/100g) content are cardioprotective factors; the dish is not deep-fried and represents a lower cardiovascular risk profile than fried rice alternatives when skin is removed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dietary fibre 3.7g/100g from whole-food sources; estimated low saturated fat (~1.2g/100g); dish is not deep-fried.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish with caution; requesting duck without skin reduces saturated fat and inflammatory load, and reducing hoisin sauce minimises sodium-related tissue swelling risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peking Roast Duck Rice (北京烤鸭饭, Běijīng kǎoyā fàn) is a popular Chinese rice dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese restaurants. It consists of steamed white rice topped with slices of Peking-style roast duck — characterised by its lacquered, mahogany-coloured crispy skin and tender, flavourful meat — accompanied by hoisin sauce, sliced cucumber, and spring onion. The duck is traditionally roasted whole in a closed or open oven at high heat after being coated with a soy-based glaze, producing a distinctive sweet-savoury flavour profile with a crisp skin and juicy interior. In the Singapore hawker context, the dish is typically served as a single-plate meal for lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate, with a notable dietary fibre content of 3.7g/100g and meaningful potassium (327mg/100g) and iron (2.55mg/100g). Sodium is 231mg/100g, which is moderate and primarily attributable to the hoisin and soy-based sauces. The estimated glycemic index is 68 (medium), moderated by the protein and fibre content relative to plain white rice. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in controlled portions; patients with hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced sauce and limit serving size. The dish is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets. It is a culturally significant dish rooted in Beijing culinary tradition, adapted for the multicultural Singapore food landscape.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical, reviewed, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The food identity is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy for roast duck adjusted for dish composition, documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database baseline for white rice, adjusted for protein and fibre moderators, and is clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #05140 (duck, domesticated, meat and skin, roasted) proportionally adjusted for the rice-to-duck ratio in this dish; HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0mg/100g in the HPB source. This is likely an underestimate for a dish containing duck meat and skin; duck meat typically contains 70–90mg cholesterol per 100g (USDA SR Legacy). The HPB value is preserved as reported but flagged for review. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific Peking roast duck rice preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white rice baseline (GI ~72) adjusted downward for protein and fibre moderators; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. The dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts as duck preparation may not follow halal slaughter standards; consumers should verify with individual vendors. 6. Aliases consistency check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
